perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information” shall mean any information, including but not limited to data, techniques, protocols or results, or business, financial, commercial or technical information, disclosed by one Party (each a “Discloser” as applicable) to the other Party (each a “Recipient” as applicable) in connection with the terms of that certain Exclusive License Agreement dated December , 2016 (the “License Agreement”) and identified as confidential at the time of disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information” shall mean any information, including but not limited to data, techniques, protocols or results, or business, financial, commercial or technical information, disclosed by one Party (each a “Discloser” as applicable) to the other Party (each a “Recipient” as applicable) in connection with the terms of that certain Exclusive License Agreement dated December , 2016 (the “License Agreement”) whether or not identified as confidential at the time of disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement that information be 'identified as confidential at the time of disclosure,' we broaden the definition of 'Confidential Information' in Section 1. This contradicts the restrictions in Section 4, which implies that only information initially designated as confidential is protected. This introduces uncertainty about what information Recipient is actually obligated to protect, as the definition in Section 1 no longer aligns with the implicit assumptions in Section 4 regarding protection only for items previously specified as confidential.
perturbation.contradicted_text: For the term of the License Agreement and a period of [***] thereafter (and indefinitely with respect to any individually identifiable health information disclosed by BIDMC to Licensee, if any), each Recipient agrees that: (i) it will not use such Confidential Information for any purpose other than as specified herein; and (ii) it will use reasonable efforts (but no less than the efforts used to protect its own confidential and/or proprietary information of a similar nature) not to disclose such Confidential Information to any other person or entity except as expressly permitted hereunder or the License Agreement. Recipient may, however, disclose Discloser’s Confidential Information only on a need-to-know basis to its and its Affiliates employees, staff members and agents (“Receiving Individuals”) who are directly participating in the Purpose and who are informed of the confidential nature of such information, provided Recipient shall be responsible for compliance by Receiving Individuals with the terms of this Agreement and any breach thereof Each party further agrees not to use the name of the other party or any of its Affiliates or any of their respective trustees, directors, officers, staff members, 19 [***] = CERTAIN CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION CONTAINED IN THIS DOCUMENT, MARKED BY BRACKETS, HAS BEEN OMITTED BECAUSE THE INFORMATION (I) IS NOT MATERIAL AND (II) WOULD BE COMPETITIVELY HARMFUL IF PUBLICLY DISCLOSED. employees, students or agents in any advertising, promotional or sales literature, publicity or in any document employed to obtain funds or financing without the prior written approval of the party or individual whose name is to be used, in the case of BIDMC such approval to be given by the Public Affairs Department. This Section 4 shall survive termination or expiration of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text defines 'Confidential Information' as information 'identified as confidential at the time of disclosure.' The changed text removes this requirement, broadening the definition to include any information disclosed, regardless of whether it was initially identified as confidential. Section 4, the contradicted text, refers to 'such Confidential Information,' implying the understanding of 'Confidential Information' used in the original definition, that it has been previously designated as such. By removing the requirement that it be initially labelled as such, the change directly contradicts Section 4's assumption that the information was originally designated as confidential, leading to uncertainty and creating an in-text contradiction. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition as the contract uses the term 'Confidential Information' with different meanings, leading to conflicting obligations.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall have the right to, and agrees that it will, use Discloser’s Confidential Information solely for the performance of its obligations and exercise of its rights under the License Agreement (the “Purpose”), except as may be otherwise specified in a separate definitive written agreement negotiated and executed between the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall have the right to, and agrees that it will, use Discloser’s Confidential Data solely for the performance of its obligations and exercise of its rights under the License Agreement (the “Purpose”), except as may be otherwise specified in a separate definitive written agreement negotiated and executed between the parties.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'Confidential Information' to 'Confidential Data' in Section 3, without defining 'Confidential Data,' it creates confusion about the scope of permissible use. Throughout the agreement, 'Confidential Information' is explicitly defined in Section 1. This change suggests that 'Confidential Data' is a different, undefined subset, creating an in-text contradiction when referencing permissible use. This affects Section 6 since ownership still refers to 'Confidential Information' but Section 3 now refers to 'Confidential Data'.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement, including without limitation all written and tangible forms thereof, shall be and remain the property of the Discloser. Upon termination of this Agreement, if requested by Discloser, Recipient shall return or destroy at Discloser’s discretion all of Discloser’s Confidential Information, provided that Recipient shall be entitled to keep one copy of such Confidential Information in a secure location solely for the purpose of determining Recipient’s legal obligations hereunder.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidential Information' to 'Confidential Data' in the modified text creates a contradiction with the contradicted text. The contradicted text explicitly refers to 'Confidential Information' as the property of the Discloser and outlines obligations related to its return or destruction. By introducing 'Confidential Data' without a clear definition, the modified text creates uncertainty about whether the same ownership and obligations apply to 'Confidential Data,' leading to a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: For the term of the License Agreement and a period of [***] thereafter (and indefinitely with respect to any individually identifiable health information disclosed by BIDMC to Licensee, if any), each Recipient agrees that: (i) it will not use such Confidential Information for any purpose other than as specified herein; and (ii) it will use reasonable efforts (but no less than the efforts used to protect its own confidential and/or proprietary information of a similar nature) not to disclose such Confidential Information to any other person or entity except as expressly permitted hereunder or the License Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: For the term of the License Agreement and a period of [***] thereafter (and indefinitely with respect to any individually identifiable health information disclosed by BIDMC to Licensee, if any), each Recipient agrees that: (i) it will not use such Confidential Information for any purpose other than as specified herein; and (ii) it will use commercially reasonable efforts not to disclose such Confidential Information to any other person or entity except as expressly permitted hereunder or the License Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'reasonable efforts (but no less than the efforts used to protect its own confidential and/or proprietary information of a similar nature)' to 'commercially reasonable efforts', a contradiction is introduced. The original text sets a benchmark for effort, while the modified text introduces a vague term like 'commercially reasonable efforts,' creating uncertainty about the level of protection required. What is 'commercially reasonable' can be subjective and interpreted differently, weakening the original obligation. This contradicts the remedies clause in Section 8, where 'irreparable harm' is invoked due to breaches since with weaker obligation, it may be difficult to prove that the breach caused irreparable harm.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each party acknowledges that any breach of this Agreement by it may cause irreparable harm to the other party and that each party is entitled to seek injunctive relief and any other remedy available at law or in equity.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'reasonable efforts (but no less than the efforts used to protect its own confidential and/or proprietary information of a similar nature)' to 'commercially reasonable efforts' weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. The original text provides a specific benchmark, while the modified text introduces a vague standard, which could be interpreted as a lower level of protection. This contradiction triggers the 'Misaligned Terminology - In Text Contradiction' definition. The contradicted text states that any breach may cause irreparable harm. With a weakened obligation in the changed text, it becomes more difficult to prove that a breach caused irreparable harm, creating a conflict between the level of protection expected and the severity of the remedy available. Therefore, a strong contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: Right to Disclose . Discloser represents that to the best of its knowledge it has the right to disclose to each Recipient all of Discloser’s Confidential Information that will be disclosed hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Right to Disclose . Discloser represents that it has the right to disclose to each Recipient all of Discloser’s Confidential Information that will be disclosed hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'to the best of its knowledge', the representation in Section 5 becomes absolute. This creates a contradiction because Discloser cannot guarantee with absolute certainty the absence of third-party rights or restrictions on all Confidential Information. This change directly contradicts the limited warranty in Section 7. Section 7 limits warranties related to intellectual property rights and Confidential Information but the change in Section 5 forces the Discloser to have absolute right to disclose, which creates uncertainty and exposes the Discloser to liability.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as granting or conferring, expressly or impliedly, any rights by license or otherwise, under any patent, copyright, or other intellectual property rights owned or controlled by Discloser relating to Confidential Information, except as specifically set forth in the License Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text limits the Discloser's representation to 'the best of its knowledge,' while the changed text removes this limitation, creating an absolute representation of the right to disclose. The contradicted text explicitly states that the agreement does not grant any rights under intellectual property rights owned by the Discloser, except as specifically set forth in the License Agreement. The change in Section 5, making the Discloser's representation absolute, contradicts the limited scope of rights granted in the contradicted text, creating a strong in-text contradiction because the Discloser cannot guarantee absolute rights to disclose if those rights are limited elsewhere in the agreement.

perturbation.original_text: All Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement, including without limitation all written and tangible forms thereof, shall be and remain the property of the Discloser. Upon termination of this Agreement, if requested by Discloser, Recipient shall return or destroy at Discloser’s discretion all of Discloser’s Confidential Information, provided that Recipient shall be entitled to keep one copy of such Confidential Information in a secure location solely for the purpose of determining Recipient’s legal obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: All Confidential Information disclosed pursuant to this Agreement, including without limitation all written and tangible forms thereof, shall be and remain the property of the Discloser. Upon termination of this Agreement, Recipient shall return or destroy all of Discloser’s Confidential Information, provided that Recipient shall be entitled to keep one copy of such Confidential Information in a secure location solely for the purpose of determining Recipient’s legal obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'if requested by Discloser, at Discloser's discretion', Section 6 now mandates Recipient to always return or destroy all Confidential Information. This directly contradicts Section 2, which lists exclusions from 'Confidential Information.' If information falls under the exclusions in Section 2 (e.g., becomes publicly available), it is unclear whether Recipient still needs to return or destroy it, leading to an inconsistency.
perturbation.contradicted_text: “Confidential Information” under this Agreement shall not include any information that (i) is or becomes publicly available through no wrongful act of Recipient; (ii) was known by Recipient prior to disclosure by Discloser, as evidenced by tangible records; (iii) becomes known to Recipient after disclosure from a third party having an apparent bona fide right to disclose it without any confidentiality obligation; (iv) is independently developed or discovered by Recipient without use of Discloser’s Confidential Information, as evidenced by tangible records; or (v) is disclosed to another party by Discloser without restriction on further disclosure. The obligations of confidentiality and non-use set forth in this Agreement shall not apply with respect to any information that Recipient is required to disclose or produce pursuant to applicable law, court order or other valid legal process provided that Recipient promptly notifies Discloser prior to such required disclosure, discloses such information only to the extent so required and cooperates reasonably with Discloser’s efforts to contest or limit the scope of such disclosure.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the phrase 'if requested by Discloser, at Discloser's discretion'. This creates a mandatory obligation for the Recipient to return or destroy all Confidential Information upon termination. However, the contradicted text (Section 2) defines 'Confidential Information' to exclude certain types of information, such as information that becomes publicly available. Therefore, the changed text creates a contradiction because it is unclear whether the Recipient is obligated to return or destroy information that falls under the exclusions listed in Section 2. This constitutes a strong in-text contradiction as defined by misaligned terminology, making it inconsistent and unclear.

perturbation.original_text: Nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as granting or conferring, expressly or impliedly, any rights by license or otherwise, under any patent, copyright, or other intellectual property rights owned or controlled by Discloser relating to Confidential Information, except as specifically set forth in the License Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Nothing in this Agreement shall be construed as granting or conferring, expressly or impliedly, any rights by license or otherwise, under any patent, copyright, or other intellectual property rights owned or controlled by Discloser relating to Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'except as specifically set forth in the License Agreement,' Section 7 now states there are absolutely NO rights granted under any intellectual property, despite the existence of the License Agreement, which would likely contain such grants. This contradicts the Purpose clause in Section 3, which implies the Recipient has rights related to the Confidential Information in order to perform its obligations under the License Agreement. This flat denial that rights have been granted when the purpose of the contract anticipates those rights leads to in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Recipient shall have the right to, and agrees that it will, use Discloser’s Confidential Information solely for the performance of its obligations and exercise of its rights under the License Agreement (the “Purpose”), except as may be otherwise specified in a separate definitive written agreement negotiated and executed between the parties.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed for exceptions to the denial of rights under intellectual property, specifically referencing the License Agreement. The changed text removes this exception, creating a blanket denial of any rights under intellectual property related to Confidential Information. This directly contradicts the Contradicted Text, which states that the Recipient has the right to use Discloser’s Confidential Information for the performance of its obligations and exercise of its rights under the License Agreement. The change, therefore, creates a clear and direct conflict, as it denies rights that the Contradicted Text implies exist, thus leading to an in-text contradiction as per the definition of 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction'.
